none: Environmental Impact Statement, Tunnel Component of the Tunnel and Resevoir Plan Proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago

The Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) discusses the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago. The plan aims to address flooding and pollution issues in Chicago by constructing a series of tunnels and reservoirs to manage stormwater and wastewater. The primary focus is on the Mainstream Tunnel System, which runs from 59th Street to Addison Street. The project is expected to improve water quality and reduce pollution in local waterways, but concerns have been raised about potential impacts on groundwater, ecology, and waste disposal.
Environmental Impact Statement, Tunnel Component of the Tunnel and Resevoir Plan Proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago

The Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) discusses the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago. The plan aims to address flooding and pollution issues in Chicago by constructing a series of tunnels and reservoirs to manage stormwater and wastewater. The primary focus is on the Mainstream Tunnel System, which runs from 59th Street to Addison Street. The project is expected to improve water quality and reduce pollution in local waterways, but concerns have been raised about potential impacts on groundwater, ecology, and waste disposal.
United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Region V
Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago (MSDGC)

The Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) was proposed to tackle chronic flooding and pollution problems in Chicago, particularly during heavy rainfall events. While the project aimed to improve water quality and manage stormwater effectively, it faced public concerns regarding its potential environmental impacts, including effects on groundwater and local ecosystems. Despite these concerns, the project has been implemented in phases and has significantly contributed to flood management in the region.
"The intense development of the area makes those few remaining relatively undisturbed areas highly valuable for fish and wildlife resources." — United States Department of the Interior

"The early resolution of the flooding and water pollution problems will improve the livability and desirability of housing in the Phase I Area." — John Waner, Director, Chicago Area Office, Department of Housing and Urban Development
